links.label: 分布于
links.evidence: Its natural habitats are subtropical or tropical seasonally wet or flooded lowland grassland, swamps, mangroves, and heavily degraded former forest. It may wander over dry fields and clearings. It is not generally found in high-altitude regions. This bird with its somehow crow-like aspect gave foot to the naming of the Quebrada de los Cuervos (Crows Ravine) in Uruguay, where they dwell together with the black vulture and the turkey vulture. File:Lesser Yellow-headed Vulture, Campos dos Goytacazes - RJ, Brasil imported from iNaturalist photo 356680714.jpg|Flock, in Rio de Janeiro, Brasil File:Lesser Yellow-headed Vulture, Pilcomayo, Formosa, Argentina imported from iNaturalist photo 478244272.jpg|With a turkey vulture, in Formosa, Argentina File:Lesser Yellow-headed Vulture, Bermejo, Formosa, Argentina imported from iNaturalist photo 475887416.jpg|With a crested caracara, in Formosa, Argentina File:Vultures at Celestún Garbage Dump - Flickr - treegrow.jpg|With a black vulture, Yucatán, Mexico

links.label: 分布于
links.evidence: When flying, it travels alone and is rarely found in groups. The flight of the lesser yellow-headed is an example of static soaring flight, which uses thermals to maintain altitude without the need to flap its wings. This vulture rarely soars high in the air, preferring low altitudes. This bird is believed to be somewhat migratory in response to the changes in water level where it lives. File:Lesser Yellow-headed Vulture, Veracruz, MX imported from iNaturalist photo 31916434.jpg|Dorsal view of wings, in Veracruz, Mexico File:Lesser yellow-headed vulture (Cathartes burrovianus) in flight.JPG|Ventral view of wings, in the Pantanal, Brazil File:Lesser Yellow-headed Vulture, Bolívar, Santander, Colombia imported from iNaturalist photo 530047421.jpg|In Colombia The lesser yellow-headed vulture, like other New World vultures, has the unusual habit of urohidrosis, in which it urinates or defecates on its legs to cool them by evaporation.{{cite book |author1 = Sibley, Charles G

links.label: 栖息于
links.evidence: It was considered to be the same species as the greater yellow-headed vulture until they were split in 1964. It is found in Mexico, Central America, and South America in seasonally wet or flooded lowland grassland, swamps, and heavily degraded former forest
